primary_diagnosis.name: Primary Sjogren's syndrome — severe sicca and systemic flare (corneal ulcer/parotitis to be excluded)

pathophysiology_summary: Primary Sjogren's syndrome is an autoimmune exocrinopathy causing severe dry eyes (keratoconjunctivitis sicca) and dry mouth, with systemic features including arthralgia/synovitis, fatigue and parotid enlargement. A severe sicca flare produces intense ocular burning, xerostomia preventing solid swallowing, and diffuse joint pain. This mimics a corneal ulcer/keratitis, bacterial parotitis and oesophageal obstruction. Reassuring features are bilateral symmetric eye involvement without sudden vision loss or a single severe photophobic eye, bilateral non-tender parotid enlargement without fever/pus, and preserved liquid swallowing. However, telehealth cannot perform a slit-lamp examination, so corneal ulceration cannot be excluded and the ocular surface is at risk overnight; the correct action is urgent optometry/ophthalmology review plus intensive preservative-free lubrication, humidification, saliva substitutes and soft/liquid nutrition — with emergency eye care for sudden vision loss or a single severely painful photophobic eye, and urgent care for a febrile unilateral hot parotid swelling.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral sicca with arthralgia on a known background.
Severe dry-eye flares risk corneal ulceration; screen vision/single-eye features.
Diffuse pain overlaps, but objective sicca/synovitis point away.
Bilateral symmetric eyes, no vision loss, non-febrile parotids, liquid swallowing preserved.
Lower probability but not excludable without a slit-lamp; overnight corneal risk remains.

differential.diagnosis: Primary Sjogren's flare — corneal ulcer to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: systemic sicca flare needing urgent optometry/ophthalmology review, intensive lubrication and nutrition support, with ocular emergency safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Sudden vision loss/marked blurring, or a single intensely painful, light-sensitive eye (corneal ulcer/keratitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sight-threatening corneal ulceration/keratitis needing emergency eye care.

red_flags.description: High fever with a single hot, red, very painful cheek/parotid or pus from the duct (bacterial parotitis)
Inability to swallow even liquids / food impaction (obstruction) or unsafe nutrition
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate obstruction or unsafe intake needing assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring a severe dry-eye flare without arranging a slit-lamp review or safety-netting ocular red flags.
Not screening for bacterial parotitis or obstruction.
Not addressing poor intake and overnight corneal exposure while isolated.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No vision loss/single severe eye; bilateral sicca — corneal ulcer not excluded without slit-lamp
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent optometry/ophthalmology review with intensive lubrication and emergency safety-netting.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Slit-lamp/fluorescein ocular examination for corneal integrity
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen ocular/parotid/swallowing red flags; arrange urgent optometry/ophthalmology (slit-lamp) review and GP review; advise intensive lubrication, humidification, saliva substitutes and soft/liquid nutrition; escalate to emergency eye care for sudden vision loss or a single severe photophobic eye, and urgent care for a febrile hot parotid.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring a severe dry-eye flare without a slit-lamp review could miss a corneal ulcer and risk vision.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage sicca with intensive lubrication and saliva substitutes; severe ocular flares require optometry/ophthalmology assessment to protect the ocular surface.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RANZCO/optometry guidance on the acute red/painful eye
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Sudden vision loss or a single severely painful photophobic eye requires emergency ophthalmic assessment.
